items.q: What are the functions of money?
items.correct: Medium of exchange, store of value, unit of account, and standard of deferred payment — money must perform these to function as money.
items.misconception: Money is valuable because it is made of valuable materials.
items.random_wrong: Money is only valuable if backed by gold.
items.why: Modern fiat money has no intrinsic value — its value comes from social acceptance and government issuance. Students think paper money 'must' be backed by something; it isn't, and hasn't been since 1971.

items.q: What components make up aggregate demand?
items.correct: AD = C + I + G + (X - M): consumption, investment, government spending, and net exports. A shift in any component shifts the AD curve.
items.misconception: Aggregate demand is just the total amount consumers want to buy.
items.random_wrong: Aggregate demand is the same as GDP measured by demand.
items.why: AD covers all spending sources, not just households. Students reduce it to consumer demand, missing that investment, government and trade are equally constitutive. Mis-attributing a shift then misdiagnoses the cause.

items.q: What is the principle of comparative advantage?
items.correct: Countries should specialise in producing goods with lower opportunity cost relative to others — even a country worse at everything still has a comparative advantage in something.
items.misconception: A country should produce whatever it can make best.
items.random_wrong: Comparative advantage means richer countries should help poorer ones.
items.why: Students confuse absolute and comparative advantage. The Ricardian insight is that mutually beneficial trade is possible even when one country is absolutely better at everything — based on opportunity cost.
It's Imports > exports. “Exports > imports” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: If the pound depreciates, UK exports become?
items.why: Correct answer: Cheaper abroad and likely rise. “More expensive abroad” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: £1 = €1.15. A French wine costs €69. How much in £?
items.why: Exact answer: 60. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
